behaviors.description: Reject webhook when `X-Signature-256` does not match raw body; do not forward event.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: failure is at module handoff (raw-body extraction + signature verifier + queue forwarder). MOCK-MISS: a unit test of only HMAC compare misses bugs where framework/body middleware changes bytes before verification. REAL-INFRA: no required real network/OS; in-process app wiring is enough. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS down-level check: Unit rejected because behavior text requires both HTTP rejection and no forwarding across modules, not single-function logic. Up-level check: System rejected because no real HTTP/network primitive is required to reproduce this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with real request parsing middleware and a fake queue module. Send a request whose header HMAC is computed for different bytes than the actual raw body. Assert status 401 and assert queue `enqueue` was not called.

behaviors.description: Persist event to queue before returning HTTP 200.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: this is ordering across handler and queue persistence interface. MOCK-MISS: unit-testing only response code misses race/order bug between `enqueue` completion and `res.send`. REAL-INFRA: real OS/network not required; controllable in-process queue adapter can expose completion timing. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS down-level check: Unit rejected because assertion is cross-module temporal ordering. Up-level check: System rejected because ordering bug is reproducible without real network/subprocess.
behaviors.test_description: Use an in-process server and instrumented persistent-queue adapter that resolves `enqueue` on demand. Send a valid webhook; verify 200 is not emitted until after `enqueue` promise resolves, then verify 200 after resolution.

behaviors.description: Return HTTP response without waiting for downstream event processing completion.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: decoupling boundary is handler -> queue write -> async processor pipeline. MOCK-MISS: single-function unit tests can miss accidental `await` on processor completion. REAL-INFRA: no mandatory real OS/network; in-process delayed processor is sufficient. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS up-level check: System considered for timing realism, but behavior text is about wait/non-wait semantics, reproducible with controlled async stubs.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with a queue and processor where processor completion is intentionally delayed. Send valid event, assert HTTP response completes before processor promise settles, while enqueue still occurs.

behaviors.description: Response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock under slow concurrent downstream processing.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 System: behavior explicitly depends on wall-clock latency under concurrency; event-loop starvation and scheduler effects are real runtime/OS phenomena. REAL-INFRA yes (timing + concurrency with real server/request path). MOCK-HIDE: fake timers/in-process stubs can pass while real runtime exceeds 3s under load. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS down-level check: Integration rejected because behavior text requires real wall-clock SLA under concurrent slow work, not just logical non-blocking.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node HTTP server process with real queue/worker configuration. Preload slow processing tasks, fire concurrent valid webhook requests, and measure end-to-end response latency; assert each response is <=3s wall-clock.

behaviors.description: Do not write duplicate event ID to queue.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: duplicate protection is state handoff between handler and persistent queue/dedupe store. MOCK-MISS: pure unit test of ID-check helper misses persistence interaction and idempotency wiring. REAL-INFRA: can be reproduced with in-process modules plus local persistent store/test DB; no required real network/subprocess. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS up-level check: System considered for race realism, but core behavior text does not require cross-process/network conditions; integration with realistic local persistence can catch real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler + real queue persistence layer against local test storage. Send same event ID twice; assert first request enqueues once and second does not create a second queue record (and returns configured idempotent response).

behaviors.description: When queue is full, return 503 and include `Retry-After`.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: failure boundary is error propagation from queue module to HTTP layer/header mapping. MOCK-MISS: unit test on error-mapping function alone can miss missing header/status translation in handler wiring. REAL-INFRA: no mandatory real OS/network. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS up-level check: System rejected because real network/subprocess not needed to surface this mapping failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue adapter that returns a typed `QueueFull` error. Send valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 plus non-empty `Retry-After` header, and no downstream processing start.

behaviors.description: Persistent queue enforces configured capacity and rejects overflow writes.
behaviors.justification: PASS1 Integration: this validates queue implementation + persistence backend configuration together (not just handler mapping). REAL-INFRA: typically local DB/filesystem persistence suffices under level definition; no required external network/subprocess. MOCK-HIDE: mocked queue always honoring capacity can hide real schema/config bugs where limits are not actually enforced. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. TWO-PASS up-level check: System considered if capacity depends on external managed queue service, but behavior text specifies persistent queue enforcement without requiring external API/OS primitives; integration remains minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue component with a small configured capacity (e.g., 2) on local test storage. Write events until limit, then attempt one additional write and assert explicit rejection/error and unchanged queue depth.
